papers.title: Chemical abundances in a high velocity RR Lyrae star near the bulge
papers.author_names: Camilla Juul Hansen Robert Michael Rich Andreas Koch Siyi Xu Andrea Kunder Hans-Guenter Ludwig

abstract.text: Low-mass, variable, high-velocity stars are interesting study cases for many aspects of Galactic structure and evolution. Until recently, the only known high- or hyper-velocity stars were young stars thought to originate from the Galactic centre. Wide-area surveys like APOGEE and BRAVA have found several low-mass stars in the bulge with Galactic rest-frame velocities larger than 350 km/s. In this study we present the first abundance analysis of a low-mass, RR Lyrae star, located close to the Galactic bulge, with a space motion of ~ -400 km/s. Using medium-resolution spectra, we derive abundances (including upper limits) of 11 elements. These allow us to chemically tag the star and discuss its origin, although our derived abundances and metallicity, at [Fe/H] =-0.9 dex, do not point toward one unambiguous answer. Based on the chemical tagging, we cannot exclude that it originated in the bulge. However, combining its retrograde orbit and the derived abundances suggests that the star was accelerated from the outskirts of the inner (or even outer) halo during many-body interactions. Other possible origins include the bulge itself, or the star could be stripped from a star cluster or the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy when it merged with the Milky Way.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: high-velocity or extreme-velocity stars

brief.tldr: [astronomical research paper] The first chemical abundance analysis of a low-mass, high-velocity RR Lyrae star near the Galactic bulge with a retrograde orbit of ∼−400 km/s reveals [Fe/H] = −0.9 dex and elemental abundances consistent with both bulge and halo origins. Despite metallicity not uniquely identifying its origin, the combination of retrograde motion and chemical composition suggests acceleration from the inner or outer halo via dynamical interactions, rather than a native bulge origin. The star's properties challenge the traditional view that high-velocity stars originate only from the Galactic center, supporting a scenario of dynamical ejection from halo or dwarf galaxy remnants.

source_fields.title: Chemical abundances in a high velocity RR Lyrae star near the bulge

source_fields.sections_text: Conclusion: The high-velocity RR Lyrae star m176, despite its extreme velocity and retrograde orbit, retains halo-like chemical compositions consistent with a bulge or halo origin, possibly through stellar interactions or tidal stripping, highlighting the need for higher-resolution spectroscopy to determine its precise formation history. Introduction: This paper innovates by using precise pulsational velocity measurements and chemical composition data from RR Lyrae stars to resolve the ambiguous Galactic component assignments—particularly distinguishing between bulge, thick disk, and halo populations—thereby addressing the problem of misclassification in metal-poor stars near the Galactic center due to overlapping metallicity distributions. Results and Discussion: The surface abundances of the high-velocity RR Lyrae star m176 are intact and unaltered, as its pulsational expansion velocity (~90 km/s) is far below its escape velocity (~231 km/s), confirming that mass loss is negligible and the chemical composition reflects its original stellar origin. Observations and data reduction: The high-resolution spectroscopic observations of MACHO 176.18833.411 were reduced using the Makee pipeline, co-added at minimum light phase to enhance signal-to-noise, and analyzed to derive chemical abundances despite the star's faintness and short integration times. Stellar parameters and abundances: Stellar parameters and abundances were determined through spectral analysis of Fe lines, with final values of T = 6600±100 K, log g = 2.0±0.3, [Fe/H] = −0.85±0.15, and ξ_micro = 4.5±0.2 km/s, derived using equivalent widths, line synthesis, and independent metallicity constraints from Ca II lines.

abstract: Low-mass, variable, high-velocity stars are interesting study cases for many aspects of Galactic structure and evolution.
Until recently, the only known high- or hyper-velocity stars were young stars thought to originate from the Galactic centre.
Wide-area surveys like APOGEE and BRAVA have found several low-mass stars in the bulge with Galactic rest-frame velocities larger than 350 km/s.
title: Chemical abundances in a high velocity RR Lyrae star near the bulge

catalog_assessment.evidence: Title and abstract describe chemical abundance analysis of a specific high-velocity RR Lyrae star using medium-resolution spectra. Paper presents derived abundances for 11 elements and discusses origin based on observational data.

papers.title: On the observability of bow shocks of Galactic runaway OB stars
papers.author_names: D. M.-A. Meyer A.-J. van Marle R. Kuiper W. Kley

abstract.text: Massive stars that have been ejected from their parent cluster and supersonically sailing away through the interstellar medium (ISM) are classified as exiled. They generate circumstellar bow shock nebulae that can be observed. We present two-dimensional, axisymmetric hydrodynamical simulations of a representative sample of stellar wind bow shocks from Galactic OB stars in an ambient medium of densities ranging from n_ISM=0.01 up to 10.0/cm3. Independently of their location in the Galaxy, we confirm that the infrared is the most appropriated waveband to search for bow shocks from massive stars. Their spectral energy distribution is the convenient tool to analyze them since their emission does not depend on the temporary effects which could affect unstable, thin-shelled bow shocks. Our numerical models of Galactic bow shocks generated by high-mass (~40 Mo) runaway stars yield H$\\\\alpha$ fluxes which could be observed by facilities such as the SuperCOSMOS H-Alpha Survey. The brightest bow shock nebulae are produced in the denser regions of the ISM. We predict that bow shocks in the field observed at Ha by means of Rayleigh-sensitive facilities are formed around stars of initial mass larger than about 20 Mo. Our models of bow shocks from OB stars have the emission maximum in the wavelength range 3 <= lambda <= 50 micrometer which can be up to several orders of magnitude brighter than the runaway stars themselves, particularly for stars of initial mass larger than 20 Mo.

source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Method: The paper proposes a 2D axisymmetric hydrodynamical simulation approach using the Pluto code to model bow shocks formed by runaway OB stars interacting with the interstellar medium, incorporating realistic stellar wind properties and radiative cooling/thermal conduction physics to resolve shock structure and evolution. Conclusion: Bow shocks from massive, slowly moving stars in dense ISM environments are best observed in infrared wavelengths, where circumstellar material dominates emission and spectral energy distributions provide stable, reliable diagnostics of their structure and evolution. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel hydrodynamical modeling approach to simulate stellar wind bow shocks in runaway massive stars, addressing the problem of accurately predicting the morphology and evolution of these structures to better understand their impact on the interstellar medium and supernova shock propagation. Bow shocks morphology: The morphology and size of bow shocks from runaway OB stars depend on stellar velocity, mass loss, and ambient ISM density, with denser media compressing the shock and potentially forming thin, unstable layers prone to non-linear instabilities. Bow shock energetics and emission signatures: This section presents the energetics and emission signatures of bow shocks from runaway OB stars, quantifying luminosities in optical, X-ray, and infrared bands across different interstellar medium densities and tracking their evolution over time.

abstract: Massive stars that have been ejected from their parent cluster and supersonically sailing away through the interstellar medium (ISM) are classified as exiled.
Independently of their location in the Galaxy, we confirm that the infrared is the most appropriated waveband to search for bow shocks from massive stars.
We predict that bow shocks in the field observed at Ha by means of Rayleigh-sensitive facilities are formed around stars of initial mass larger than about 20 Mo.

Our models of bow shocks from OB stars have the emission maximum in the wavelength range 3 <= lambda <= 50 micrometer which can be up to several orders of magnitude brighter than the runaway stars themselves
, particularly for stars of initial mass larger than 20 Mo.
title: On the observability of bow shocks of Galactic runaway OB stars

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper presents hydrodynamical simulations of bow shocks from runaway OB stars. Focus is on theoretical modeling, predictions of observability, and spectral energy distributions. No actual observational stellar data or catalog presented.
